name: Unnamed Location (Serpentine)
short_description: A serpentine rockhounding site near Blocher in Saline County. Collect banded green and white serpentine specimens on gently sloping terrain suitable for beginning rockhounders.
description:
This unnamed serpentine locality in Saline County, Arkansas offers rockhounders the chance to collect distinctive green and banded serpentine specimens. The site is characterized by gentle slopes that make access relatively easy compared to many Arkansas rockhounding locations. Serpentine in this region formed through metamorphic alteration of olivine-rich ultramafic rocks during Precambrian mountain-building events that affected the Ouachita orogen. The soft, often silky texture of serpentine makes it highly desirable for polishing and lapidary work.

The terrain here supports typical Arkansas woodland vegetation with gentle topography that does not require strenuous hiking. The geological setting reflects the complex structural history of central Arkansas, where serpentine bodies occur as metamorphic rocks interlayered with schists and quartzites. Specimens found here range from translucent apple-green to mottled white and dark green banding.

This is a good introduction to Arkansas rockhounding for families and beginning collectors. The relatively accessible terrain and abundant material make it suitable for a half-day outing. Bring standard tools for breaking and collecting specimens, and be prepared for typical Arkansas humidity and occasional wet conditions depending on season.
history: Serpentine deposits in the Saline County area formed during Precambrian metamorphism within the Ouachita orogen, where intense pressure and temperature altered original olivine-rich ultramafic oceanic crust into the soft, green metamorphic rock found here today. These metamorphic rocks are part of the broader Arkansas slate belt that extends across central Arkansas and records ancient continental collisions.
tips: Search along gentle slope faces and areas where natural weathering has exposed fresh green serpentine outcrops. Use a hammer and chisel to break away specimens from the bedrock, targeting areas with distinct banding and clear green coloration. Look for translucent pieces that will polish well - avoid heavily weathered brown or gray surfaces which indicate alteration. Bring a small saw or rotary tool if you plan to cut and shape specimens on site.
terrain_notes: Gentle rolling terrain with typical Arkansas woodland vegetation including hardwoods and understory brush. Elevation approximately 600-700 feet. Terrain is relatively even with no steep grades, making foot access straightforward. Walking distance from parking area is short to moderate. Footing is generally stable though natural leaf litter and roots may require careful stepping.
written_directions: From Blocher, take local roads east approximately 2-3 miles toward the GPS coordinates in Saline County. The site is accessed via unpaved county roads or property roads. Consult current maps and verify access with local property owners before visiting, as land status in this area may be private.
road_conditions: Unpaved roads, possible washboard or ruts depending on recent weather. High-clearance vehicle recommended.
parking_notes: No formal parking area. Park along road edge or designated pullout areas. Verify with property owner before parking.
collection_rules: Access and collection rules depend entirely on land ownership and current access agreements. Contact Saline County or local property owners to confirm current collection rights and any restrictions. Private land - permission required.
rules:
Contact property owner for permission before collecting
Verify current access status with Saline County
Remove all trash and litter
Close gates behind you
Respect posted boundaries
nearest_services: Blocher has limited services. Benton, Arkansas (approximately 10-15 miles west) offers gas, food, and lodging.
accessibility_notes: Gentle sloping terrain accessible to most hikers with reasonable fitness. No wheelchair access to collecting areas. Unpaved roads to site. Sturdy footwear recommended for uneven natural terrain. Not suitable for those with significant mobility limitations due to unpaved roads and natural terrain.

faq.question: What can I find rockhounding for serpentine near Blocher, Arkansas?
faq.answer: This site yields green and banded serpentine specimens suitable for lapidary work and polishing. Pieces range from translucent apple-green to white and dark green banded varieties. The soft nature of serpentine makes it ideal for beginners learning cutting and polishing techniques.

faq.question: Is there a fee to rockhound serpentine in Saline County, Arkansas?
faq.answer: This is private land with no established fee rockhounding operation. You must obtain permission from the property owner before collecting. Contact Saline County or local landowners to confirm current access and collection rights.

faq.question: Do I need a permit to collect serpentine in Arkansas?
faq.answer: No state permit is required for personal rockhounding on private land with owner permission. However, since this site is on private property, you must contact the landowner first and follow any collection guidelines they specify.

faq.question: When is the best time to rockhound for serpentine near Blocher?
faq.answer: The ideal season is March through May or September through November when temperatures are moderate and the ground is not waterlogged from Arkansas summer rain. Avoid hot summer months and late fall/winter when conditions may be muddy.

faq.question: What tools do I need to collect serpentine specimens at this Arkansas site?
faq.answer: Bring a geology hammer, cold chisel, and small saw for breaking and shaping specimens. A headlamp or flashlight helps inspect cracks and fracture planes. Leather gloves protect hands from sharp fragments, and a collection bag keeps specimens organized.

faq.question: Is this serpentine rockhounding site good for beginners and families?
faq.answer: Yes, the gentle terrain and easy access make this suitable for families and new rockhounders. Serpentine is soft enough for children to help break specimens with supervision, and the site requires minimal hiking ability. Always confirm property access and bring adequate water and shade.

meta_description: Collect green and banded serpentine specimens near Blocher in Saline County. Easy terrain, beginner-friendly. Permission required on private land.
